2000-04-01.title: In a Dhaka Slum, the Grisly Fate of 'Handcuff' Babul
2000-04-01.abstract: Residents of teeming and miserable slum in Dhaka, Bangladesh, are relieved that man who had extorted money from them, known as Handcuff Babul, has had his arms chopped off with machete; he could not have operated without blessing of politically well-connected mobsters known as mastans, and rumor is that he angered one of them, leading to his punishment; photos (M)

2000-04-01.title: World Briefing
2000-04-01.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: RIGHTS OFFICIAL VISITS    The United Nations human rights chief, Mary Robinson, arrived in Moscow on her way to inspect a detention camp in Chechnya where prisoners are said to have been beaten and tortured by Russian soldiers. Mrs. Robinson is also due to meet with top Russian security officials, but so far no meeting with President-elect Vladimir V. Putin is planned.   Celestine Bohlen (NYT)    SWITZERLAND: AUSTRIAN ON TRIP  Chancellor Wolfgang Schussel of Austria was welcomed with full diplomatic honors in Bern on his first official foreign trip. European Union countries have ostracized Austria's new right-wing government, and Mr. Schussel said, ''I hope the Swiss behavior will persuade other European countries to think twice about what they're doing.'' The visit, a traditional one for new Austrian chancellors, was met with protests by Jewish, anti-racism and left-wing groups.   Elizabeth Olson (NYT)

2000-04-01.title: Albright Drops Objection to Loan Deal for Russia Oil Company
2000-04-01.abstract: Secretary of State Madeleine K Albright says she has dropped her objections to $500 million in loan guarantees from Export-Import Bank to Tyumen, Russian oil company that has been accused of fraud; Tyumen wants money to hire two American companies to rehabilitate oil field and modernize refinery; State Dept official says Tyumen is close to resolving fraud charges, and that company has been privatized (M)

2000-04-01.title: In Mexico, Top Newsman And Network Part Ways
2000-04-01.abstract: Jacobo Zabludovsky, Mexico's most influential television newsman, parts company with Televisa, extraordinarily powerful network he has worked for for decades; incoming prime-time anchor, Joaquin Lopez Doriga, says he will remain impartial in reporting on presidential campaign now under way; this marks sharp change from Zabludovsky's coverage of last presidential race, in which he was openly favorable to government; muted reaction to Zabludovsky's resignation is itself sign of times in Mexico, authoritarian one-party state that is making its way toward democracy (M)

2000-04-01.title: Security Council Votes to Let Iraq Buy Oil Gear
2000-04-01.abstract: United Nations Security Council votes unanimously to allow Iraq to import $1.2 billion in spare parts and other equipment for its ramshackle oil industry, so that more oil can be pumped efficiently and safely to pay for civilian goods and public service projects; Iraq has been allowed to sell unlimited quantities of oil since December, although earnings are monitored and expenditures controlled through UN escrow account; third of profits goes to pay claims arising from Iraq's 1990 invasion of Kuwait, which led to international sanctions against Pres Saddam Hussein (M)

2000-04-02.title: Zimbabwe Protesters Are Beaten by Government Supporters
2000-04-02.abstract: Armed men wielding machetes and clubs, described by witnesses as supporters of Pres Robert Mugabe, attack people in peaceful anti-government march in Harare, leaving several people seriously injured; marchers claim attackers seemed determined to go after handful of whites in crowd; clash between Mugabe supporters and opponents comes as Zimbabwe braces for its most heated parliamentary election since independence in 1980; Mugabe's party, which has governed for 20 years, faces its most serious challenge ever (M)

2000-04-02.title: Fujimori's Shadowy Aide Highlighted by Election
2000-04-02.abstract: Vladimiro Montesinos, architect of Pres Alberto K Fujimori's re-election drive, is accused by opponents of running campaign of dirty tricks and has himself become issue in race; opposition newspaper recently obtained detailed information showing that Montesinos has in recent years had annual income of more than $2 million, refueling old charges, never proved, that he is in pay of Peruvian drug traffickers; Montesinos has been crucial ally of Clinton administration in efforts to stop drug trafficking in Andean region, and American officials do not mention him by name in public criticisms of current election campaign; Montesinos has become such a political liability for Fujimori that political analysts wonder why Fujimori does not dismiss him; critics say it is because Montesinos, who was Fujimori's personal lawyer, knows too much about Fujimori's personal life and finances; photo (M)

2000-04-02.title: Feeling Pinch, Japan's Mobs Struggle for Control
2000-04-02.abstract: Organized crime in Japan is feeling pinch of weak economy and aggressive crackdown by police and community groups; this has sparked bitter and often violent struggle among rival gangs for control of shrinking criminal enterprises (M)

2000-04-02.title: Rebel Ambush In Chechnya One of Worst For Russians
2000-04-02.abstract: Russian Defense Ministry reports that as many as 32 Russian commandos died when their convoy was attacked in rebel ambush in Zhani-Vedeno in Chechnya; attack is one of worst in widening partisan war, and is second ambush against elite unit since attack in Grozny left 20 dead (M)

2000-04-02.title: Uganda Cult's Mystique Finally Turned Deadly
2000-04-02.abstract: Ugandan authorities say death toll has risen to at least 924 people among members of Movement for Restoration of Ten Commandments of God, making it highest death toll of any cult in modern history; police now speculate that some cult members rebelled and asked for their money and belongings back when world did not end on Jan 1, as cult leaders had prophesized; photos; map (M)

2000-04-03.title: Britain Is Ready to Take a Bet on Privatization : Air Traffic Control PLC?
At a Beijing Aquarium, Dolphins Are Hostages
2000-04-03.abstract: Chinese and American partners in newly built Beijing Aquarium are involved in contract dispute so vicious that United States Embassy has intervened; JV China, California firm, accuses its Chinese partners of endangering animals and failing to meet contractual agreements; JV has suspended its dolphin shows, saying aquarium is months behind in payments and has skimped on promised life support systems; aquarium, denying danger to fish, has tried to evict JV China; as China seeks entry into World Trade Organizstion, this conflict is seen as cautionary tale about perils of business in China, where rule of law and standard business practices have not fully taken hold (M)

2000-04-03.title: Beirut Journal; U.S. Angel of Mercy Stayed for a Merciless War
2000-04-03.abstract: Gladys Mouro is one of few Americans working at American University of Beirut who did not leave Lebanon amid hostage-taking, assassinations and threats that marked that country's civil war in 1980's; Mouro's book, An American Nurse Amidst Chao, describes how war made medical center in effect not only field hospital, but also target; Mouro, who is now assistant director of hospital is revered in Lebanon; photo (Beirut Journal) (M)

2000-04-03.title: Divided Plaintiffs Must Now Cooperate
2000-04-03.abstract: With the collapse of talks between the government and Microsoft, the Justice Department and the 19 state attorneys general who brought the antitrust case must now cooperate to devise a single remedy proposal.

2000-04-03.title: Analysts See Risk for Microsoft, But Gates Hopes to Win Appeal
2000-04-03.abstract: Industry analysts say collapse of settlement talks means Microsoft Corp will face harsh legal road ahead that threatens to embolden private litigants, depress company's stock price and erode its dominant influence in computing; Microsoft's chairman, William H Gates, says company plans to battle antitrust case in courts through appeals for years, if necessary; Microsoft clearly feels that course is preferable to offering further concessions in mediation efforts; company's plight could be altered by outcome of presidential election, since Gov George W Bush's view of antitrust enforcement is more limited than Clinton administration's; Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson's expected finding of monopoly power and abuse of that power will make it easier for companies and class-action lawyers to bring cases against Microsoft; company is confident it can win those case; photo (M)

2000-04-04.title: Despite a Stricken Leader, Japan Keeps a Level Head
2000-04-04.abstract: Nikkei 225 stock index closes at 20,726.99 on April 3, its highest level in three years, despite news that Prime Min Keizo Obuchi has been hospitalized in serious condition (M)

2000-04-04.title: With Japan's Premier in a Coma, Succession Moves Get Under Way
2000-04-04.abstract: Prime Min Keizo Obuchi is in coma, kept alive on respirator, after suffering stroke on April 2; Japan does not learn gravity of his illness until nearly 36 hours after he is hospitalized; Mikio Aoki, chief cabinet secretary, assumes temporary control of government; political analysts predict that country will hold elections before July; leading candidates for prime minister are Foreign Min Yohei Kono and Yoshiro Mori, secretary general of ruling Liberal Democratic Party; photos (M)

2000-04-04.title: Beijing Court Nullifies Conviction of Stanford Researcher
2000-04-04.abstract: Beijing High People's Court, in unusual judicial reversal, nullifies conviction of Hua Di, Chinese-born Stanford University researcher who has been in prison for over two years for 'revealing state secrets'; Hua, former Chinese missile scientist, was sentenced to 15 years in prison for academic publications in US about China's military program; photo (M)

2000-04-04.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-04-04.abstract: AFRICA    ZIMBABWE: PROTESTING MUGABE  An opposition group vowed to begin 100 days of prayer meetings and peaceful demonstrations to protest the policies of President Robert Mugabe and his governing party. The decision by the National Constitutional Assembly came after thugs attacked its peaceful march over the weekend, injuring several people. The group, which blamed the police for failing to prevent the violence, has criticized official corruption, the unraveling economy and Mr. Mugabe's support of black squatters on white-owned farms.    Rachel L. Swarns (NYT)    AFRICANS AND EUROPEANS MEET  African leaders pleaded in Cairo for sweeping relief of their continent's crippling debt at the start of the first Africa-Europe summit meeting. President Hosni Mubarak of Egypt, left, opened the talks between the 15-nation European Union and the 52-nation Organization of African Unity with a call for a radical solution to the debt problem. Despite economic reform and efforts to spread democracy and human rights, he said, Western aid had slumped in the last two decades and Africa's share of trade had shrunk.    (Reuters)    SOUTH AFRICA: UNIONS' GLOBAL VIEW  The world labor union movement said it would muster all its strength to fight the social and economic fallout of globalization, which it said was widening the gap between rich and poor nations. Labor leaders addressing the start in Durban of the 17th World Congress of the International Confederation of Free Trade Unions said globalization needed to pay attention to social rights for it to be a sustainable economic system.    (Reuters)    MIDDLE EAST    IRAQ: TURKS STRIKE AT KURDS  Turkish F-16 jets struck Kurdish rebel bases in northern Iraq, as more than 1,000 troops penetrated some six miles into the region in a new campaign to hunt down the guerrillas, a military official said. Cobra helicopter gunships were reported reinforcing the troops and armored units. No serious clashes with the guerrillas were reported.    (AP)    ASIA    INDONESIA: SUHARTO QUIZZED  A five-member team from the attorney general's office questioned former President Suharto at his home about corruption during his 32-year rule. The session ended after 90 minutes when Mr. Suharto said he was tired. He has defied summonses to appear at the attorney general's office for questioning, saying he is too ill.   Seth Mydans (NYT)    LAOS: BLAST TIED TO TERRORISTS  Authorities blamed unspecified terrorists for an explosion at the Thank You Very Much Restaurant in the capital, Vientiane, that it said wounded six Western tourists and two Laotians. Such acts are rare in the capital but bandits and the remnants of anti-Communist Hmong insurgents do operate in the countryside.    Seth Mydans (NYT)    AFGHANISTAN: CURRENCY WEAKENED  Reported drought damage to opium crops has further battered Afghanistan's weak currency and driven money changers in Kabul to try to dump the local currency in favor of hard currencies. A bumper harvest last year established Afghanistan as the world's leading source of opium, the United Nations estimates. Afghanistan relies on opium, the raw ingredient for heroin, as its primary export.    Christopher S. Wren (NYT)    THE AMERICAS    MEXICO: VOTE SPLITS PARTY  A primary in the race for governor of Tabasco State has revived bitter divisions in the governing party, known as the PRI, with complaints that the departing governor, Roberto Madrazo, rigged the vote to favor a longtime aide, Manuel Andrade, over Congressman Arturo Nunez, who has spent years as a senior official in the government and the PRI. Partial results showed Mr. Andrade with 57 percent of the vote and Mr. Nunez with 38 percent. In the presidential primary last year Mr. Madrazo challenged the PRI establishment, losing to Francisco Labastida.    (AP)    EUROPE    SWITZERLAND: HOLOCAUST TRIAL  A Swiss man who denies that Hitler's gas chambers existed has gone on trial on charges that he violated Switzerland's anti-racism law. Gaston-Armand Amaudruz, 79, is being tried in Lausanne for writing that there is no evidence of the Holocaust's gas chambers. A 1995 Swiss law makes it illegal to deny or justify genocide. Mr. Amaudruz, a spokesman for a far-right group, faces up to three years in jail and a $25,000 fine.    Elizabeth Olson (NYT)    IRELAND: EX-PREMIER PAYING BACK TAXES  Former  Prime Minister Charlie Haughey, 74, will sell off family properties to pay more than $1.2 million in back taxes, including interest, on gifts received while in office from a supermarket executive. Mr. Haughey's fall from grace reached a low last year when he was booed by spectators at the funeral of a previous Fianna Fail party leader. He is also facing charges of obstructing a 1997 tribunal looking into payments to politicians.    Brian Lavery (NYT)    GERMANY: RIGHTIST DATA  The number of neo-Nazi and right-wing extremists declined from about 54,000 in 1998 to around 51,000 in 1999, the Federal Agency for the Protection of the Constitution said in an official report. The number of rightists deemed likely to commit acts of violence, however, grew by about 10 percent to 9,000, the agency said, adding that recorded violent attacks by far rightists reached 746, up from 708 in 1998. Germany's seven million foreigners are considered particular targets of those attacks.    Victor Homola (NYT)    GERMANY: SPYING ON KOHL  The former head of East Germany's foreign espionage service, Markus Wolf, said in an interview with Reuters that his agents had known for a long time about the illegal slush fund set up by the Christian Democrats under former Chancellor Helmut Kohl. But he said he made little use of the information because he considered all capitalists to be corrupt.    Victor Homola (NYT)

2000-04-04.title: 7 Villagers in Kashmir Slain During Anti-Police Protest
2000-04-04.abstract: Seven demonstrators against recent killings of villagers by police are killed in Kashmir and at least another four are wounded; are part of march of about 1,000 people from their villages to district police headquarters in Barakpura; photo (M)

2000-04-05.title: Swiss Charge Dictator's Ally In Plundering Of Nigeria
2000-04-05.abstract: Swiss authorities bring first charges in case involving at least $654 million in bank accounts linked to late Nigerian dictator Sani Abacha, his family and associates; Nigeria's new elected government seeks return of money looted by Abacha (S)

2000-04-05.title: South Korean Women's Movement in Politics:How Big a Revolution?
2000-04-05.abstract: SEOUL—  They cheer loudly at rallies and zealously round up votes for their favorite candidates, but South Korean women are virtually powerless when it comes to voting on legislation, setting national policy or turning the wheels of bureaucracy.

2000-04-05.title: World Briefing
2000-04-05.abstract: AFRICA    UGANDA: INVESTIGATING CULT DEATHS -- Several hundred members of a doomsday cult who died in a fire in a church last month were still alive when the flames broke out, forensic tests show. The doors and windows of the chapel were bolted from the outside, findings of the preliminary investigation confirm. ''It was an attack from the inside,'' A. B. M. Lugudo, deputy commissioner of Uganda's forensics agency, told The Associated Press. ''We are still looking to see if these people started the fire and tried to run away, but got caught up in the fire.''   Ian Fisher (NYT), (AP)

2000-04-05.title: In a Russian Region Apart, Corruption Is King
2000-04-05.abstract: Entrenched corruption in Russian city of Kaliningrad described; former Konigsburg, seized from Germany during World War II, was closed Soviet city, and is now separated from rest of Russia by 300 miles of Lithuania and Belarus; Gov Leonid P Gorbenko is considered law unto himself, with personal control of lucrative dealings in cigarettes, liquor and autos; photos; map; city has deep harbor, well-educated labor force, amber deposits, rich farmland and endless beaches, but foreign investors have fled since Gorbenko's election in 1996 (M)

2000-04-05.title: U.N. Says Bad Government Is Often the Cause of Poverty
2000-04-05.abstract: Report by UN Development Program points to bad government as major cause of third-world poverty, and calls for making 'good governance' top priority in fighting poverty; new tack follows years of treading carefully around issue of why certain countries remain poor even in regions of substantial economic growth and political freedom; message is not likely to please third-world leaders looking for more aid and debt relief; program's new administrator Mark Malloch Brown, from Britain, explains (M)

2000-04-06.title: Agencies Urge Use Of Affordable Drug For H.I.V. in Africa
2000-04-06.abstract: World Health Orgn and United Nations AIDS program say 23 million Africans infected with virus that causes AIDS should be given regular doses of common and affordable antibiotic to ward off fatal secondary infections; recommendation is based on encouraging preliminary studies with drug, best known as Bactrim; Clinton administration is spending more than $100 million to fight AIDS abroad and wants to raise that to $325 million in next year's budget; Bactrim has been prescribed for years in wealthy countries to ward off AIDS-related infections (M)

2000-04-06.title: Iran Says It Seized Ship Smuggling Iraqi Oil
2000-04-06.abstract: Iranian government says it seized tanker illegally exporting Iraqi oil as it passed through Iranian waters; two weeks ago, United States warned that increased oil smuggling from Iraq could not be stopped without Iran's help; spokesman for State Dept welcomes news, if confirmed, of Iran's cooperation (M)

2000-04-06.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-04-06.abstract: THE AMERICAS    PARAGUAY: ARGENTINA TO EXTRADITE TWO  An Argentine court ordered the extradition of two Paraguayans accused in the assassination of Vice President Luis Maria Argana of Paraguay last year. The men, arrested in January, were named by a man who confessed to being an accomplice in the killing, which led to the overthrow of Paraguay's president and forced its armed forces chief, who was accused of ordering the crime, into exile. Lawyers said the two men would appeal.    Larry Rohter (NYT)

2000-04-06.title: Tommaso Buscetta, 71, Dies; First Italian Mafia Informer
2000-04-06.abstract: Tommaso Buscetta, first important Italian mob leader to break Mafia oath of silence and whose testimony helped convict hundreds of Mafia leaders in Italy and US, dies at age 71; photo (M)

2000-04-06.title: Turkish President Rebuffed on a Second Term
2000-04-06.abstract: Turkish Parliament rejects constitutional amendment that would have allowed Pres Suleyman Demirel to seek second term; Demirel will have to step down next month and be succeeded by new president chosen by Parliament; vote is defeat for him and also for Prime Min Bulent Ecevit, who campaigned hard for Demirel (M)

2000-04-07.title: Xialing Journal; For the Up-to-Date Village, a One-of-a-Kind God
2000-04-07.abstract: Local folk religions have spread all over China in recent years; across rural China there has been renaissance of idiosyncratic home-grown religions; although China's leaders have spent much of last year denouncing cults and superstion, in part to justify their ban on Falun Gong spiritual movement, they have mostly turned blind eye toward abundance of folk religions that have re-emerged as a focus of rural life; China has four officially sanctioned religions--Buddhism, Taoism, Christianity and Islam--and praying to spirits or deities is officially ridiculed and strongly discouraged; still, local authorities often have strange symbiosis with indigenous temples, which have become powerful economic and social forces; photo; map (L)

2000-04-07.title: List Adds Fuel to Anti-Corruption Campaign : Seoul Names Politicians With Criminal Records
2000-04-07.abstract: SEOUL—  The National Election Commission added ammunition Thursday to the nationwide civic action movement with the revelation that some candidates for the National Assembly have criminal records.

2000-04-07.title: Foe's Strength Forces Putin Into a Humbling Reversal
2000-04-07.abstract: Russia Pres-elect Vladimir V Putin asks Valentina Matviyenko, his first deputy prime min, to pull out of race for governor of St Petersburg, race she had entered with Kremlin's blessing less than month ago; Putin move is seen humbling concession to political strength and popularity of incumbent governor, Vladimir Yakovlev; it is seen galling retreat for Putin; analysts say that Putin cannot afford battle with strong regional leader like Yakovlev while preparing to undertake economic reforms; photo of Putin aboard nuclear submarine (M)

2000-04-07.title: Beirut Says It Can't Prevent Palestinian Attacks on Israel
2000-04-07.abstract: Officials in Beirut, in new sign of Arab unease, warn they could not, and would not, prevent Palestinian militants from using Lebanese territory to mount attacks on Israel after its army withdraws unilaterally from southern Lebanon; Lebanon Pres Emile Lahoud says 'daily miniwars' could break out along border with Israel unless United Nations disarms Palestinian refugees living in miserable camps scattered across Lebanon--and unless it actively prevents Israeli retaliation against any Palestinian operation (M)

2000-04-07.title: World Briefing
2000-04-07.abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: ARMY BASE BOMBED  A bomb exploded inside a British Army base near Londonderry, demolishing a guardhouse wall but causing no casualties. It was the third attack on security forces in two months, all attributed to republican groups at odds with the Irish Republic Army. Mitchel McLaughlin, chairman of the I.R.A.'s political wing, Sinn Fein, and a member of the Assembly suspended in February, said, ''There is a very clear correlation between political failure and the activities of these groups because they sustain themselves by that failure.''   Warren Hoge (NYT)    RUSSIA: EUROPEAN WARNING  The Council of Europe in Strasbourg, France, has voted to start suspension proceedings against Russia unless it calls a cease-fire or starts talks with rebel leaders in Chechnya by May. No country has ever been suspended from the 41-nation consultative body, and the Russian delegation walked out after the vote. Officials said in private it was unlikely member countries would take up the recommendation. But the vote was intended to put pressure on President-elect Vladimir V. Putin.   Suzanne Daley (NYT)    FRANCE: ENGLISH ORDER DROPPED  Air France suspended an order forcing its pilots to speak English with air traffic controllers at Roissy-Charles de Gaulle airport in Paris. It denied it acted in response to political pressure, and said the rule could be reinstated after a review. The requirement had drawn criticism from French language purists and government officials.   (AP)    AUSTRIA: APOLOGY OVER EX-NAZIS  The Social Democrats, in opposition after decades in power, apologized for providing a political haven to former Nazis after World War II. The party's new leader, Alfred Gusenbauer, said it must be ''deeply ashamed'' of the way it welcomed ex-Nazis to its ranks. Several served in the cabinets of the former Social Democatic chancellor, Bruno Kreisky; others joined the rightist Freedom Party now led by Jorg Haider.   Roger Cohen (NYT)    GERMANY: RACISM CHARGE  The campaign slogan of a leading Christian Democrat, Jurgen Ruttgers, has been criticized as racist by a European Union watchdog committee. Mr. Ruttgers has been campaigning in North Rhine-Westphalia with the slogan ''Children not Indians'' -- a derisive reference to a proposal that skilled Indians should be allowed to fill jobs in Internet-related industries. ''German politicians should have learned more than others from history,'' the European Monitoring Center on Racism said.   Roger Cohen (NYT)    AFRICA    ZIMBABWE: LOOKING TO BRITAIN  Parliament approved an amendment to the Constitution requiring Britain, the former colonial power, to compensate white landowners for any property seized by the government. The vote was rejected by Britain, which said one state could not impose constitutional obligations on another. The clause, which was included in a draft charter rejected by voters in February, allows the government to take white-owned land without paying for it.   Rachel L. Swarns (NYT)    UGANDA: CULT LEADER WARRANTS  The police issued warrants for the arrest of six leaders of a doomsday cult that the authorities say killed at least 924 of its members. It is not known if any of them are still alive after a church fire in Kanungu, southwest Uganda, on March 17, in which as many as 530 members died. Among those wanted are Joseph Kibwetere, leader of the Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments, and Credonia Mwerinde, who claimed to have had visions of the Virgin Mary.   Ian Fisher (NYT)    MOZAMBIQUE: CYCLONE ALERT  A cyclone closed in on Mozambique, drenching its northern region and setting off a scramble to prepare for a possible new natural disaster, just weeks after hundreds died in floods. The United States sent in disaster aid teams to deal with possible damage.   Henri E. Cauvin (NYT)    THE AMERICAS    CANADA: REFUGEE BILL  The government introduced a bill in Parliament intended to speed the processing of refugee claims, to better screen claimants for criminal records, to streamline deportation procedures and to impose fines of up to $700,000 and life in prison for anyone convicted of smuggling illegal immigrants. The bill comes after boatloads of illegal Chinese arrived in Vancouver last year.   James Brooke (NYT)    ASIA    INDIA: CASTE LEADER HELD  Laloo Prasad Yadav, left, the charismatic peasant caste leader in the state of Bihar, was placed in judicial custody for two weeks after being charged with having more assets than can be explained by his income. His wife, Rabri Devi, Bihar's chief minister, was given bail, though she faces the same charges. The judge said, ''She like any Indian housewife was under pressure of her husband, so she committed abetment.''   Celia W. Dugger (NYT)    MIDDLE EAST    ISRAEL: SETTLERS EVACUATED  Soldiers evacuated an encampment of Jewish settlers, including leaders of the movement, from a hilltop in the West Bank. The settlers were protesting a High Court decision to allow Palestinian cave dwellers to return to their neighboring homes as part of what they said would be an increase in protest activities against the policies of Prime Minister Ehud Barak.   Deborah Sontag (NYT)    IRAN: CLARIFICATION ON JEWS  A spokesman for the judiciary said that not all of the 13 Jews charged with spying for Israel face death or life imprisonment. Hossain Sadeqi said Iranian law provided for lesser punishments in some spying cases. The 13 Jews, and six Muslims arrested with them, are due to face trial on April 13, but court officials have hinted at a possible delay.   John F. Burns (NYT)    IRAQ: 14 REPORTED KILLED  Fourteen people were killed by Western air strikes in southern Iraq, the official news agency said, quoting a military spokesman. The United States military's central command, in Tampa, Fla., said Navy and Marine Corps attack jets and British Tornadoes struck antiaircraft artillery targets after ''repeated antiaircraft fire'' against jets patrolling the southern no-flight zone.   (Reuters)

2000-04-08.title: Israel Plans a Test for Wagner
2000-04-08.abstract: Israel Symphony Orchestra announces that it will include Richard Wagner's Siegfried Idyll in chamber music series in fall; Wagner was Nazis' favorite composer and symbol of anti-Semitism; Israeli musical establishment had ignored Wagner because it did not want to offend Holocaust survivors; photo (M)

2000-04-08.title: Cambodia's Mountains Hide a Wildlife Refuge
2000-04-08.abstract: Scientists are discovering treasure trove of rare plants and species in Cardamom Mountains of Cambodia now that civil war is over; mountains were last enclave of Khmer Rouge guerrillas; map; photos (M)

2000-04-08.title: As Zimbabwe Falters, Doubts About Who Is Really to Blame
2000-04-08.abstract: Pres Robert Mugabe finds himself and his party at crossroads as Zimbabwe celebrates two decades of independence and they brace for national elections; many blacks blame him, not white minority, for nation's dramatic economic decline; percentage of people living in poverty has surged from 40 percent to over 63 percent in last decade, and wages have fallen to pre-independence levels; map; photo (M)

2000-04-08.title: World Briefing
2000-04-08.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: LORD ARCHER ARRESTED  The millionaire novelist and peer Jeffrey Archer was arrested and released on bail in connection with a sexual scandal that forced him to resign last fall as the Tory candidate in next month's London mayoral election. Lord Archer, 59, stepped down when it emerged that he had persuaded a friend to lie in court in his $800,000 libel case in 1987 against The Daily Star. The paper, which said he had sex with a prostitute, wants its money back.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

2000-04-08.title: On a Visit to Moscow, Monetary Fund Official Is Bullish on Putin's Program
2000-04-08.abstract: International Monetary Fund acting managing dir Stanley Fischer, after meeting Pres-elect Vladimir V Putin in Moscow, says fund is ready to pursue new relationship with Russia as soon as Putin acts on his promise to build better foundation for country's still shaky market economy (M)

2000-04-09.title: Clinton to Meet Separately With Barak and Arafat
2000-04-09.abstract: Pres Clinton will meet in Washington separately with Prime Min Ehud Barak of Israel and Yasir Arafat, Palestinian leader, hoping to reinvigorate their stalled peace talks (M)

2000-04-09.title: Angolan Paradox: Oil Wealth Only Adds to Misery
2000-04-09.abstract: Angola's oil wealth has helped pay for endless war between Pres Eduardo dos Santos and his bitter foe, Jonas Savimbi, while most Angolans live in increased poverty and ill health; Angola has world's highest rate of citizens maimed by land mines; one third of children die before age 5; life expectancy is just 42 years; while most Angolans live in misery, country's rulers spend oil earnings on weapons and are thought to be stealing part of what they do not spend on weapons; international banking officials say $900 million in oil revenues was not recorded in country's published budget; American officials concede that because US needs Angola's oil, they hesitate to criticize Angolan government or demand too much, too fast; American oil executive says consumers are more interested in gasoline than Angola's social problems; photo; map (M)

2000-04-09.title: Cloud on Fujimori's Future Shadows Voting in Peru
2000-04-09.abstract: Pres Alberto K Fujimori of Peru faces his most serious political crisis since he shut down Congress and Supreme Court at height of guerrilla war in 1992; 14 million voters will decide whether he should serve third term in office, and seveal leading pollsters say first-round victory for him is unlikely unless he resorts to fraud; diplomats and experts say campaign has been so marred by dirty tricks and legal irregularities that Fujimori will emerge weakened leader, even if he wins; challenge to Fujimori is lead by Alejandro Toledo, who has capitalized on growing concerns about campaign as well as grumbling over three years of particularly bad economic hardship; photo (M)

2000-04-09.title: U.S. Resists Bid To End Tariffs For 3rd World
2000-04-09.abstract: Clinton administration opposes World Bank proposal that would increase exports from world's poorest and most indebted nations for fear it would inflame sentiments in Congress against Pres Clinton's efforts to win passage of African trade bill and to give China permanent normal trading status; propsal pressed by World Bank and International Monetary Fund would allow poorest nations in world to sell goods to United States and other Western nations without facing any tariffs or import quotas; this would go far beyond administration's efforts to get Congress to forgive their debts as they undergo economic reform (M)

2000-04-09.title: Europe's Dim View Of U.S. Is Evolving Into Frank Hostility
2000-04-09.abstract: Growing backlash of anti-Americanism is evident around Europe; Europeans are talking about America as menacing, even dangerous force intent on remaking world in its image; many members of Europe's political, cultural and intellectual elite use kind of moral calculator to deplore American model as severely wanting; poking fun at America has always been European pastime, but experts say today's criticism has virulence and element of fear never seen before; fall of Berlin Wall leaves America as only superpower, generating fear that strength of America's economy will impose not only economic changes but social changes as well; Europeans are reading menace in wide range of recent events; photos (M)

2000-04-10.title: Foes in Congo Appeal For U.N. Peacekeepers
2000-04-10.abstract: All sides in war in Congo appeal for United Nations to deploy peacekeepers swiftly following signing of latest cease-fire in 18-month-old conflict; factions for and against Pres Laurent Kabila appear militarily weary and financially pressed (M)

2000-04-10.title: Rape of Mexican Teenager Stirs Abortion Outcry
2000-04-10.abstract: Feminist groups and some prominent intellectuals in Mexico take up cause of teenage rape victim who was dissuaded from abortion by doctors and by senior state officials, even though abortion is legal for rape victims in Mexicali, where she lives; no official ever refused her the abortion, but they counseled her against it so openly and forcefully that she and her family became frightened and desisted; as many as half million women terminate their pregnancies clandestinely each year in Mexico; photo (M)

2000-04-10.title: Socialist Premier in Greece Is Re-elected by Thin Margin
2000-04-10.abstract: Greek voters narrowly re-elect Socialist prime miniser, Costas Simitis, whose mandate was jolted by Costas Karamanlis, fresh but untested politician; Simitis brought faltering Greek economy into line with European Union, but results are not ringing endorsement he sought when he called early elections; virtual dead heat reflects voters' thirst for change rather than any deep ideological differences; Karamanlis espouses same pragmatic path taken by Simitis; photo (M)

2000-04-10.title: Indonesia Strengthens Navy to Unify and Protect Its Islands
2000-04-10.abstract: Jakarta — President Abdurrahman Wahid, in his policy speech after being elected in October, promised to enlarge and strengthen the Indonesian Navy, which was long treated by former President Suharto as a poor relation to the politically powerful army.

2000-04-10.title: Epidemic of a Cattle Disease Inflicts a Toll on South Korea
2000-04-10.abstract: Livestock markets throughout Far East are upset by news of outbreak in South Korea of foot-and-mouth disease, one of most destructive cattle diseases known; South Korea has taken increasingly muscular measures to prevent spread of extremely contagious viral disease; it poses no threat to humans; last confirmed outbreak, in Taiwan in 1977, forced slaughter of over three million pigs; map (M)

2000-04-11.title: Iraqi Doctor, Once on C.I.A.'s Payroll, Fights to Stay in U.S.
2000-04-11.abstract: Ali Yassin Mohammed Karim, doctor who worked for CIA in Iraq but was detained by US immigration officials after being spirited into country for protection, will fight for political asylum in US instead of being sent to neutral country; is contesting government assertions that he is national security threat with ties to Iraq, Syrian or Iranian intelligence operations; has been held in detention facility in California for past three years (M)

2000-04-11.title: China Rejects U.S. Actions On Bombing Of Embassy
2000-04-11.abstract: China rejects official US explanation of bombing of its embassy in Belgrade during NATO air war against Yugoslavia in 1999 and again demands 'thorough and comprehensive' investigation; criticizes CIA's dismissal of midlevel officer blamed for attack as inadequate and reiterates demands that Clinton administration punish those responsible; US declares matter closed (M)

2000-04-11.title: Tokyo Chief Starts New Furor, on Immigrants
2000-04-11.abstract: Shintaro Ishihara, governor of Tokyo, sets off swell of criticism in diplomatic circles and foreign communities here by saying that immigrants in Japan, mainly people of Korean and Chinese descent, are likely to riot after major earthquake and calls on military to be prepared to maintain order in such eventuality; refers to immigrants as 'sangokujin,' derogatory term used after country was defeated in World War II to tell Korean and Chinese residents to leave (M)

2000-04-11.title: Woman Sets Out to Lead Kohl's Party Out of Its Crisis
2000-04-11.abstract: Angela Merkel, who was raised in Communist system, takes control of Christian Democratic Union, conservative movement that has dominated postwar German politics but now faces crisis over its finances and its identity; is overwhelmingly elected leader by party delegates and is first woman and first easterner chosen since party was founded after World War II; pledges new beginning; photo (M)

2000-04-11.title: PERUVIAN'S LEAD IN VOTE PROMPTS CHARGE OF FRAUD
2000-04-11.abstract: Official returns show Pres Alberto K Fujimori approaching first-round victory in Peru's presidential election, prompting cries of fraud and threats of civil disturbance; National Election office reports Fujimori leading challenger Alejandro Toledo 49.6 percent to 40.6 percent with 56.4 percent of vote counted; Fujimori's closeness to outright majority, coupled with unexplained delays of up to several hours in getting ballot boxes and tally sheets to computer centers, some of which were shut down for more than day, have led to suspicions of fraud; Eduardo Stein, head of observer team from Organization of American States, expresses misgivings; says something sinister has happened; Fujimori rejects charges of fraud; says vote is totally normal and just; Toledo calls for annullment of election results; ballots have already been destroyed as allowed by Peruvian law; photos (M)

2000-04-12.title: Belgrade Stepping Up Intimidation Of Journalists
2000-04-12.abstract: Yugoslav government steps up campaign to intimidate and impoverish independent press as hundreds attend rites honoring Slavko Curuvija, journalist gunned down on Belgrade street year ago after turning against Pres Slobodan Milosevic's regime; has been using courts to impose heavy fines on media to spur self-censorship (M)

2000-04-12.title: Unions Prepare to Hit the Street in Washington
2000-04-12.abstract: American labor unions, worried that world economy is destroying many good jobs, are descending on Washington for protests against China trade bill and to back students, environmentalists and religious groups protesting globalization; effort comes as labor's leadership has hardened opposition to liberalized trade and its ties with Clinton administration have frayed; photo (M)

2000-04-12.title: World Briefing
2000-04-12.abstract: EUROPE    KOSOVO: POSITIVE SIGN -- Ending an almost four-month boycott, Kosovo Serbs sent a representative to observe a session in Pristina of the province's interim government, in a sign they are ready to renew cooperation with rival ethnic Albanians. The United Nations-supervised meeting was attended by Bernard Kouchner, the United Nations administrator of Kosovo, as well as the ethnic Albanian leaders Ibrahim Rugova and Hashim Thaci. The observer for the Serbs was Rada Trajkovic, above.   (AP)

2000-04-12.title: Lebanon Chafing as Syria's Partner
2000-04-12.abstract: Syrian Pres Hafez al-Assad continues to pulls strings in fractious Lebanon 24 years after Syrian troops arrived to end civil war; many Lebanese chafe under often heavy-handed Syrian dictates, while admitting that nation desperately needs Syria as economic partner; relationship remains lopsided, although Syrians have grown more subtle and Lebanese more open in desire to define ties in recent years; some 30,000 Syrian troops are based in Lebanon, buttressed by unknown number of intelligence agents; map; photo (M)

2000-04-12.title: Charging Fraud, Peruvian Challenger Demands Runoff
2000-04-12.abstract: Alejandro Toledo, Peruvian Pres Alberto K Fujimori's main rival, calls for election runoff after monitors challenge incomplete tally that puts Fujimori close to 50% needed to avert runoff; says he will take part only if electoral agencies are revamped; Pres Clinton's spokesman says United States would raise 'serious questions' if runoff is not held; photo (M)

2000-04-13.title: World Briefing
2000-04-13.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: CAPITALIST ADVICE  An independent-minded pro-capitalist economist will serve as adviser to President-elect Vladimir V. Putin, Mr. Putin's spokesman said. Mr. Putin has yet to outline a detailed economic program. But his selection of Andrei Illarionov, 38, is a sign he will follow a program of free-market reforms. Mr. Illarionov has criticized Russian dependence on the International Monetary Fund.   Michael Gordon (NYT)

2000-04-13.title: Lott Assures Colombian President on $1.6 Billion to Fight Drugs
2000-04-13.abstract: Pres Andres Pastrana of Colombia wins assurances from Senate majority leader, Trent Lott, that Congress will approve $1.6 billion in aid to help train and equip Colombian security forces to fight drug war, but not until late spring or early summer (M)

2000-04-13.title: Israeli High Court Orders Release of 8 Lebanese Prisoners
2000-04-13.abstract: Israel's Supreme Court reverses itself, ruling that Israel is not justified in holding Lebanese detainees as bargaining chips for missing Israeli soldiers; court orders that 8 of 15 Lebanese detainees who have been imprisoned without charges for more than decade be released immediately; after court rules, Israeli prison authorities say they will release 13 of 15 detainees; two others, Mustafa al-Dirani and Sheik Abdel Karim Obeid, are high-ranking guerrilla leaders who are being held in secret location by army; Justice Min Yossi Beilin says government would try to retain them as security risks; Prime Min Ehud Barak expresses his opposition to prisoner release but says he would abide by court's ruling; photo (M)

2000-04-13.title: Plan for Summit Buoys Kim's Election Hopes in South Korea
2000-04-13.abstract: Pres Kim Dae Jung's political fortunes improve considerably at home after announcement of his planned meeting with North Korean leader, Kim Jong Il; for first time in campaign, political experts are saying Kim's party could win in midterm parliamentary elections, or at very least stave off debilitating defeat (M)

2000-04-13.title: Deal for Early-Warning Plane Hangs Over Jiang's Arrival in Israel
2000-04-13.abstract: Pres Jiang Zemin of China arrives in Israel, but United States opposition to planned Israeli arms deal with his country overshadows first official visit by Chinese leader there; Jiang will hold talks with Prime Min Ehud Barak, who reportedly told Americans that Israel will provide Chinese with just one advanced plane, although it agreed to give them option to purchase at least three more; Congressional leaders in Washington have threatened to cut military aid to Israel in retaliation for sale (M)

2000-04-14.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-04-14.abstract: THE AMERICAS    CANADA: FRENCH RULING  A Quebec court ruled that the government of the mainly French-speaking province has the right to limit use of English and to impose French as the dominant language on commercial signs. In reversing a lower court decision, Justice Paul-Marcel Bellavance of the Superior Court noted that Quebec's six million French speakers are surrounded by 300 million English speakers, saying, ''Quebec is in a unique linguistic situation in the world.''   James Brooke (NYT)    COLOMBIA: LIMITED TRUCE  The second-largest leftist guerrilla group, the Army of National Liberation, has announced a limited 10-day Easter truce and said it would release the four-member crew of an Avianca Airlines flight hijacked a year ago. The truce will apply only to civilian targets.   Larry Rohter (NYT)

2000-04-14.title: Serbia's Bickering Opposition Plans Rally Against Milosevic
2000-04-14.abstract: Serbia's political opposition, superficially united after months of squabbling and pressure from West, will hold major political rally in Belgrade on April 14 but with no hope of pushing Pres Slobodan Milosevic out of power anytime soon (M)

2000-04-14.title: Leader's Party Gains Seats in South Korean Vote, as Do Rivals
2000-04-14.abstract: South Korea's governing Millenium Democratic Party, buoyed by diplomatic breakthrough with North Korea, picks up additional seats in mid-term parliamentary elections; although it fails to win plurality, Pres Kim Dae Jung's party performs better than expected (M)

2000-04-14.title: A New Age Protest Tackles Globalism With Polite Chants
2000-04-14.abstract: Global economy protesters start to arrive in Washington, where they hope over weekend to somehow block World Bank and International Monetary Fund meetings; marchers seeking attention take to streets, where police shepherd them and stop traffic; protesters denounce 'capitalism, corporate greed'; stalled motorists are irate (M)

2000-04-14.title: Lawmakers In Japan Hear Grim Sex Case
2000-04-14.abstract: Japanese lawmaker Yoshihide Sakaue, senior member of Parliament's Special Committee on Juvenile Issues, peppers government authorities with question about how they had dealt with accusations that Japan's leading talent agent, Johnny Kitagawa, had sexually abused teenage boys he was grooming for stardom; case reviewed (M)

2000-04-15.title: Large Crowd in Belgrade Heartens Yugoslav Opposition
2000-04-15.abstract: At least 100,000 people jam central Belgrade for opposition rally demanding early elections to oust government of Pres Slobodan Milosevic; gathering is first opposition rally in many months, and first since 1997 to bring together Vuk Draskovic and Zoran Djindjic, rival leaders of main opposition parties; size of crowd likely to be registered by Milosevic, who said that did not have real opposition but only traitorous politicians acting at behest of Western countries that waged war against Yugoslavia year ago; photo (M)

2000-04-15.title: Putin Wins Vote in Parliament On Treaty to Cut Nuclear Arms
2000-04-15.abstract: Pres-elect Vladimir V Putin, in first initiative as Russia's elected president, wins approval of long-delayed Start II nuclear arms reduction treaty, which calls for cutting of American and Russian arsenals by half; 228-131 approval by lower house reflects more centrist makeup of new Parliament and diminished influence of Communist bloc, which opposed treaty; passage is seen as sign that Putin is seeking more constructive relationship with West, but also that he intends to resist US program to develop and deploy antimissile defenses; he warns Russia's willingness to carry out treaty depends on Washington's continued adherence to 1972 Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty, which sharply restricts testing and deployment of antimissile systems; pact is strongly supported by Russian military, which lacks resources to support huge nuclear arsenal developed by former Soviet Union;  Parliament's move is welcomed by Clinton administration; Pres Clinton calls it giant step toward safer future; says both countries should seize opportunity to intensify discussions on Start III and ABM treaty; photos (M)

2000-04-15.title: Deal Reached on Bill to Help African and Caribbean Trade
2000-04-15.abstract: House and Senate leaders reach broad agreement on bill to give African and Caribbean countries wide-ranging trade benefits to help flagging economies by opening new markets: agreement comes after Speaker J Dennis Hastert and majority leader Sen Trent Lott personally intervened to resolve deadlock, but many details remain unsettled; bill would reduce or eliminate tariffs and quotas on wide range of goods; supporters voice confidence over final approval; Pres Clinton calls it win-win proposition for US, Africa and Caribbean Basin; some of its provisions noted (M)

2000-04-15.title: U.S., Welcoming Russian Approval of Nuclear Arms Treaty, Says It Hopes for Further Cuts
2000-04-15.abstract: Clinton administration officials welcome Russia's ratification of Start II, nuclear arms reduction treaty, saying it sets stage for Pres Clinton and Pres-elect Vladimir V Putin to meet to try to hammer out grand bargain for next stage of arms control; say Putin has demonstrated ability to deal with Parliament and are pleased with his statement to lawmakers that Russia can ill afford arms race; Pres Clinton says approval opens way to further arms cuts, specifically through intensified talks on Start III and on changes to 1972 Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty, which Russia resists (M)

2000-04-15.title: Hunt for Holocaust Victims' Property Turns in New Direction: Toward Israel
2000-04-15.abstract: Commmitte of Israeli legislators led by Collette Avital is trying to determine what happened to untraced assets of Holocaust victims in Israel, troubled legacy of Holocaust that has gone unexplored for decades; inquiry reflects growing sense in Israel that country should practice what it preaches; Israel has demanded, with significant measure of success, that foreign banks and governments disclose possible holdings of accounts and other property of victims and survivors so assets can be returned to relatives; photo (M)

2000-04-16.title: Months Later, Mud Victims In Venezuela Still Lack Aid
2000-04-16.abstract: Venezuelan Government aid has been slow in arriving to victims of Dec mudslides and floods that inundated 25-mile strip of Caribbean coast for reasons ranging from political squabbling to lack of organization; victims, tired of waiting, are beginning self-help efforts; national government says it wants to relocate many of displaced people to interior and rebuild disaster zone's tourist facilities, but money for program is scarce and opposition strong (M)

2000-04-16.title: C.I.A. Tried, With Little Success, to Use U.S. Press in Coup
2000-04-16.abstract: Internal Central Intelligence Agency report shows CIA officials planning 1953 coup in Iran tried, with little success, to plant articles in American newspapers saying Shah Mohammed Reza Pahlevi's return resulted from homegrown revolt against Communist-leaning government; says none of Americans covering coup worked for CIA; analysis of press coverage shows American journalists filed straight-forward factual dispatches, but did not mention that some of street violence was stage-managed by CIA agents posing as Communists and gave little stress to accurate reports in Iranian newspapers that Western powers were secretly arranging shah's return to power (M)

2000-04-16.title: Kazakhstan Leader Says Albright Is Meddling
2000-04-16.abstract: State Sec Madeleine K Albright, visiting Kazakhstan, complains that Pres Nursultan A Nazarbayev gave new posts to aides dismissed in connection with sale to North Koreea of 40 MIG fighters, news conference; Nazarbayev complains of United States meddling (M)

2000-04-16.title: American Effort to Isolate Belgrade Falters
2000-04-16.abstract: Western diplomatic quarantine of Serbia has broken down, with every major European nation represented in Belgrade by senior diplomats; United States has no diplomats in Belgrade and no plans to send any as long as Pres Slobodan Milosevic remains in power, but has local staff of about 50 people; Europeans seek to distinguish between isolation of government and Serbian people, who still see selves as Western-oriented; tension between Clinton administration and European Union over efficiency of sanctions and how to bring down Milosevic surfaces regularly (M)

2000-04-16.title: High-Level Fumbling Led to Chechen Rout of Russian Unit, Survivors Say

2000-04-17.title: Israeli Official May Face Sex Assault Charges
2000-04-17.abstract: Israeli police recommend that Transportation Min Yitzhak Mordechai be prosecuted on three counts of sexual assault, dealing humiliating blow to former general who ran for prime minister last year as first Sephardic candidate in country's history; Mordechai had stepped down temporarily when police launched their probe; he says he is innocent, but that he will resign from cabinet if charges are filed against him; 23-year-old woman who worked as his subordinate brought charge; when investigation became public, police received many other similar allegations from women who worked for him during his army career and subsequently his political life; photo (M)

2000-04-17.title: Chinese Embassy Bombing: A Wide Net of Blame
2000-04-17.abstract: Detailed investigation of missteps that led to bombing of Chinese Embassy in Belgrade last May finds wider circle of blame than government's explanation of simple error of judgment by few people at Central Intelligence Agency (special report: Fateful Choice); officials say bombing resulted from error piled upon incompetence piled upon bad judgment in variety of places--from frantic rush to approve targets to questionable reliance on inexpert officers to inexplicable failure to consult people who might have averted disaster; say bombing could have been avoided at several points along way; see no evidence that bombing was intentional; detailed chronology of events; photos; map; chart (L)

2000-04-17.title: From Cell, Coalition Looked Like a Movement
2000-04-17.abstract: Mike Saltz, 22-year-old student at University of Oregon, is one of 600 people swept up in mass arrest of demonstrators near International Monetary Fund in Washington, DC; Saltz spent Saturday night and Sunday morning in holding cell before paying $50 fine and being freed; he says it gave him time to ponder his part in weekend's street protests in Washington, in diffuse movement for 'economic justice,' and police reaction to it; photo (M) photo (M)

2000-04-17.title: Ulster Unionist Leader Disavows Militants
2000-04-17.abstract: David Trimble, Ulster's most prominent Protestant politician, says he would rid his party's governing council of members of traditionally militant Orange Order, in determined move to free himself from growing internal opposition to Northern Ireland peace agreement; Orange Order, whose parades of marchers have convulsed Northern Ireland in recent summers, opposes peace accord, and has backed recent attempts to overthrow Trimble's moderate leadership of Ulster Unionist Party (M)

2000-04-17.title: I.M.F. Points to Big Accomplishment: It Met as Scheduled
2000-04-17.abstract: World finance ministers hold full day of meetings to discuss global financial stability and to set course for International Monetary Fund; their one definitive accomplishment is that they met at all, considering demonstrations going on in streets surrounding IMF building; delegates got predawn wake-up calls so they could arrive ahead of demonstrators (M)

2000-04-18.title: World Trade Officials Pledging To Step Up Effort Against AIDS
2000-04-18.abstract: World finance ministers and central bankers, trying to show sensitivity to poverty as protests against World Bank and International Monetary Fund meeting wind down in driving rain, Washington, DC, pledge to pay more attention to globalization's victims and to commit 'unlimited money' to fight AIDS in poor nations; hold World Bank should intensify its fight against AIDS because disease is as much problem of poverty as public health; pledge to accelerate debt relief for developing world and press rich nations to agree to import certain products from poorest ones duty free; United States waters down language on duty-free market access to bar Congressional backlash; Mayor Anthony A Williams says police handled protests properly, news conference; protests close down normal business in city, with many city and federal workers told to stay home and many streets closed, but are unable to disrupt IMF and IBRD meetings; World Bank president James D Wolfensohn complains IBRD is being accused of causing poverty when its workers are devoted to easing poverty; map; photo (M)

2000-04-18.title: U.N. Council to Review Sanctions Policy as Criticism Increases
2000-04-18.abstract: United Nations Security Council, faced with growing criticism of embargoes that fail to deter dictators but often hurt civilians, decides unanimously to weigh how sanctions are applied and how they can be improved; Robert Fowler, council president, says working group, drawing on outside experts, will begin six-month review; move reflects wider sanctions review taking place around world; Sec Gen Kofi Annan cites growing distrust of sanctions and their ability to bring about change at fair cost (M)

2000-04-18.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-04-18.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    ISRAEL: PRISONER PLEA REJECTED  The Supreme Court rejected a last-minute appeal that sought to prevent the release of Lebanese detainees who have been held as ''bargaining chips'' for missing Israeli soldiers. They will now be freed by midweek, the government said, but the cabinet and Parliament will meet in special sessions today to contemplate legislation to bypass the court's decision that holding detainees as ''bargaining chips'' is illegal.   Deborah Sontag (NYT)    ISRAEL: 5 CONVICTED IN BRIDGE COLLAPSE  Three years after four Australian athletes were killed and scores injured when a footbridge collapsed during the Maccabiah Games, five Israelis were convicted of causing death and injury by negligence. The five, who face four years in prison, include an engineer, two contractors, the director of a special events company and the organizer of the games.   Deborah Sontag (NYT)

2000-04-18.title: GEPHARDT OPPOSES CHINA TRADE BILL
2000-04-18.abstract: House Democratic leader Richard A Gephardt, in setback for Clinton administration, reportedly plans to oppose granting China permanent normal trading privileges; is expected to free House Democrats to vote their consciences and best interests of their districts; House vote is considered too close to call (M)

2000-04-18.title: Mindful of China, U.S. Agrees To Weapons Deal for Taiwan
2000-04-18.abstract: Clinton administration, seeking to avoid further roiling China-Taiwan tensions, agrees to sell Taiwan upgraded versions of three missiles and long-range radar system, but not four technology-laden destroyers; national security adviser, Samuel R Berger, Defense Sec William S Cohen and Deputy State Sec Strobe Talbott agree on plan at White House meeting; some senior Republicans, including Sen Jesse Helms, call for more robust arms deal (M)

2000-04-19.title: World Briefing
2000-04-19.abstract: EUROPE    ITALY: PREMIER LOOKING TO QUIT -- With Prime Minister Massimo d'Alema appearing intent on announcing his resignation to Parliament today after losing regional elections on Sunday, his shaken coalition was ponderering whom to propose to try to form a new center-left majority. If Parliament does not approve a candidate, President Carlo Azeglio Ciampi will either appoint a caretaker government or call elections, now likely to favor the big winner in the regional vote, former Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi, above, leader of the center-right.   Alessandra Stanley (NYT)

2000-04-19.title: U.N. Warns It Will Enforce Angola Trade Ban
2000-04-19.abstract: United Nations Security Council threatens possible sanctions against violators of international sanctions on trading with Angola's Unita rebel movement head Jonas Savimbi; will review in Nov data on violations being amassed by international monitors; nations cited in recent report on sanction violators have scrambled to reply to charges and, in some cases, to curtail illegal trading (M)

2000-04-19.title: Will the New Economy Leave Australia Behind?
2000-04-19.abstract: SYDNEY—  Living Up to Expectations/Last of a series

2000-04-19.title: Zimbabwe Opposition Leader Warns Against Confrontation
2000-04-19.abstract: Zimbabwe opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai, in Washington, DC, seeks support for free and fair national elections; warns against making Pres Robert Mugabe into martyr; calls for conciliation as way to undercut Mugabe's push for confrontation (M)

2000-04-19.title: Tories Trying To Top Labor In Toughness On Asylum
2000-04-19.abstract: British Conservative Party leader William Hague, stepping up attacks on Labor government's alleged softness toward refugees, holds next Conservative government will detain all new asylum seekers in secure centers and deport them quickly if claims are rejected, speech; Home Sec Jack Straw holds Conservatives cynically exploit issue; he and other government officials seek to present Labor as party with tougher asylum policy (M)

2000-04-20.title: Radical Environmentalist Convicted of Gas Well Blast in Canada
2000-04-20.abstract: Canada's most famous environmental radical, Wiebo A Ludwig, is convicted of bombing gas well in 1998 and encasing another wellhead in concrete; ruling by provincial judge, Sterling Sanderman, also convicts Ludwig of three additional explosives-related charges; Ludwig, former evangelical pastor, used his eight-week trial to lambaste Alberta's oil and gas industry for callousness toward environment; Ludwig's arrest ended wide probe of rash of 160 acts of vandalism against oil industry in northwestern Alberta (M)

2000-04-20.title: New Victories in Parliament Show Strong Hand of Putin
2000-04-20.abstract: Pres-elect Vladimir V Putin has scored back-to-back parliamentary victories three weeks after his election, proof that he has political strength to command majority in both houses; it is not clear what he plans to do with his mandate; his first major policy statement is expected soon after his May 7 inauguration (M)

2000-04-20.title: Italy's Center-Left Prime Minister Resigns After a Dismal Election
2000-04-20.abstract: Prime Min Massimo D'Alema resigns following his coalition's humilating defeat in regional elections; D'Alema had headed Italy's 57th government since World War II; Silvio Berlusconi, center-right opposition leader, insists that Pres Carlo Azeglio Ciampi dissolve Parliament right away and call early elections, rather than name someone to govern until scheduled elections year from now (M)

2000-04-20.title: Amid 11th-Hour Wrangling, Israel Releases 13 Lebanese
2000-04-20.abstract: Israeli soldiers release 13 Lebanese detainees after Israeli Supreme Court rejects 11th-hour petition to keep them behind bars; men have spent years in prison without charges, held as 'bargaining chips' for missing Israeli soldiers; released men are mobbed by welcoming friends and relatives at Israeli-Lebanese border; photo (M)

2000-04-20.title: Genetics Offers Denouement To Mystery of Prince's Death
2000-04-20.abstract: Two scientists say they have proof that disease-riddled boy who died in 1795 after years alone in prison cell in France really was Louis XVII, son of Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette; more than 500 books have been written about young dauphin, and many theories have emerged about his possible escape and presumed exile after his parents were sent to guillotine at height of French Revolution; would-be heirs have popped up all over Europe through the years; scientists, Jean-Jacques Cassiman and Bernd Brinkmann, both specialists in human genetics, examined remains of boy--a dried heart--and claim they matched his DNA with that of other members of royal family, including DNA from lock of Marie Antoinette's hair; photo (M)

2000-04-21.title: China Admits Banned Sect Is Continuing Its Protest
2000-04-21.abstract: China's state-controlled media for first time acknowledges government's persistent difficulty in stamping out banned Falun Gong spiritual movement, even as fresh allegations emerge about police brutality toward its practitioners under detention; human rights groups report that three more jailed members of group have died in last month as result of beatings or hunger strikes; this brings total reported deaths to 15; official New China News Agency's admission that Falun Gong members are holding silent protests in Tiananmen Square almost daily is stunning news (M)

2000-04-21.title: Atlantic Crossing / A 'Little' Excitement and a Lot of Grit : Windsurfer Is Bearing Down on Her Dream
2000-04-21.abstract: PARIS—  Everything considered, Raphaela le Gouvello said above the slapping of the waves and the crackling of a satellite phone, her trans-Atlantic trip had gone well. Forget the sharks, the whales, the seasickness, the loneliness, the time her food ran out and she had to be resupplied by a French Navy vessel out of Guyana — forget, above all, the freakish lack of winds that threatened her dream of becoming the first woman to cross the ocean solo on a windsurfer.

2000-04-21.title: Bid to Disqualify a Key Mexican Candidate
2000-04-21.abstract: Hotly contested race for mayor of Mexico City, second most powerful post in Mexico, is turned upside down by legal challenge to credentials of leftist candidate who is front-runner, Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador; Pres Ernesto Zedillo's party and two others sue to disqualify Lopez Obrador on ground that he has not lived in city for five years, as federal election laws require; experts say challenge is likely to prevail in court; Lopez Obrador has considerable backing in city's vast shantytowns; he accuses authorities of seeking to eliminate him as voice for poor and mobilizes backers for street demonstrations and informal vote on whether to let him run; photo (M)

2000-04-21.title: Sergei Zalygin, 86, Is Dead; Editor Fought Soviet Censors
2000-04-21.abstract: Sergei Zalygin, writer and editor of literary journal Noy Mir who in late 1980's published long-banned works by Boris Pasternak, Joseph Brodsky and Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn, dies at age 86; photo (M)

2000-04-21.title: OBITUARY : Lila Kedrova Howard, Actress
2000-04-21.abstract: LONDON—  SAULT STE. MARIE, Ontario (AP) — Lila Kedrova Howard, who won an Oscar award and a Tony award for her role as Madame Hortense in the film and stage version of "Zorba the Greek," has died of heart failure.

2000-04-22.title: Colombians Flee Into Panama as War Fears Rise
2000-04-22.abstract: Hundreds of Colombians, caught up in country's civil war, flee to dense jungles in Darien province, Panama; so too have have guerrillas from Revoultionary Armed Forces of Colombia and paramilitary squads searching for refugees suspected of giving them help; arrival has placed sudden burden on Panamanian officials who, lacking resources, have turned for help to international relief agencies and Roman Catholic Church; unsettling question is whether chaos and violence will spill over unsecured border, especially if American program to help Colombia battle narcotics trafficking escalates country's civil war; map; photos (M)

2000-04-22.title: Argentina Holds 14 Officers in Beating of Labor Demonstrators
2000-04-22.abstract: 14 police officers are arrested in Buenos Aires, Argentina, for beating 35 union supporters and shooting another during crackdown on demonstration that has jeopardized Pres Fernando de la Rua's economic policy; incident, in which officers bludgeoned demonstrators as they lay on ground, is one of worst cases of police brutality in Argentina since civilians retook power from military dictatorship in 1983; several hundred labor supporters demonstrate to block access to Congress building to impede start of Senate debate on new labor code aimed at lowering labor costs and making businesses more competitive on world markets with measures including reducing power of national unions to set wages in local businesses (M)

2000-04-22.title: RUINED EAST TIMOR AWAITS A MIRACLE
2000-04-22.abstract: Little has changed in ruined East Timor as United Nations passes six-month mark in its first experiment in building a new nation; whole towns and villages still stand burned, roofless and silent, devastated by rampage of destruction of that followed East Timor's vote last August to end 24 years of Indonesian occupation; as many as 80 percent of territory's 700,000 people still have no jobs; another 100,000 or more remain in camps across border in Indonesian West Timor, still afraid to return; desperation of East Timor's unemployed, and first spasms of violence it has spawned, are sharpest signs of swelling discontent; aid workers and diplomats say they fear that this discontent could lead to lawlessness and political disarray and could open door to trouble from Indonesian-backed militias that crossed border to Indonesian West Timor after laying waste to territory last September; there is still no working police force or justice system, no government structure, few schools, no working water or power or transportation system, no post office, not much of an economy, little reconstruction; scene in Dili described; map; photos (M)

2000-04-22.title: New Cyber Galleries Hope to Lure Wider Clientele : Betting on Online Market
2000-04-22.abstract: NEW YORK—  Fine art and antiques are finding their way out of traditional bricks and mortar galleries and auction houses and onto the Web, and dealers, investors and entrepreneurs are rushing to capitalize on the projected burgeoning growth of the online art market.

2000-04-22.title: G.O.P. Senators Tell Clinton They Oppose Him on ABM Treaty and Defense System
2000-04-22.abstract: Senior Republican senators, including Trent Lott and Jesse Helms, tell Pres Clinton they oppose his efforts to renegotiate ABM Treaty of 1972 and to build only limited antimissile defense system; letter complains that Clinton would tie hands of future administration by allowing Russia to dictate changes in treaty, which bars any national antimissile defense system (M)

2000-04-23.title: In a Land of Want, an Expensive War
2000-04-23.abstract: Ethiopia is waging expensive war against Eritrea, estimated to cost $500,000 per day in salaries alone, while its eight million people struggle through worst food shortage in nearly 20 years; experts say while prime culprit is three years of poor rains, war has diverted attention and resources, complicating solutions and providing point of soreness that has distracted both Ethiopian officials and rich outside nations that would help alleviate hunger; situation has prompted difficult questions over whether outside nations are obligated to help country that is spending its own money on costly war even as its residents starve; map; photo (M)

2000-04-23.title: New Testimony Gives a Push to Irish Corruption Investigations
2000-04-23.abstract: Frank Dunlop, lobbyist and former press officer with Irish government, admits paying local politicians equivalent of $136,000 to win approval for shopping mall, giving new life to corruption investigations that have dragged on fruitlessly for nearly three years; lists 15 Dublin politicians to whom he paid cash near homes or offices (M)

2000-04-23.title: A Tower of Babel for East Timorese as They Seek a Language
2000-04-23.abstract: Newly independent East Timor, which endured 400 years of Portuguese rule and 24 years of Indonesian occupation, struggles with highly emotional issue of choosing national language; about 60 percent of population speaks local Tetun and as many as 90 percent of those under 35 speak Indonesian, as do 40 percent of those over 35; only 10 percent speaks Portuguese; announcement by government leaders that Portuguese will be official language has spurred heated debate since move would make linguistic outsiders of great majority of East Timorese, pushing them to sidelines of nation al reconstruction; those most affected would be young with education and skills,who are most vital to building nation; photo (M)

2000-04-23.title: At Extra-Special Time, Jubilation in Jammed Rome
2000-04-23.abstract: Despite shiny veneer of modern comfort and efficiency, Holy Year 2000 Jubilee in Vatican during Holy Week is laced with same kind of humble piety and awe that drew Jubilee pilgrims to Rome in 1300; photo (M)

2000-04-23.title: The Andes in Tumult, Shaken by Political Tremors
2000-04-23.abstract: Recent discontent dividing Andean nations of Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru and Bolivia differs from conflicts of past, being generated less by disagreements over type of government than by quality; urgent need for more effective governments is converging with desire that governments be democratic and longing for more representative and responsive governance is now greater than ever before among region's 100 million people; aspiration increasingly bumps up against governments that have been unable to provide equitable sharing of power and wealth; situation especially in Venezuela and Peru discussed; map; photo (M)

2000-04-24.title: Life in South Hard for North Koreans
2000-04-24.abstract: South Korean government officials say 900 North Koreans who defected to South Korea over past 20 years have failed to adapt to life in free world, despite generous financial aid, job training and other assistance; their difficulties illustrate deep disparities between two Koreas since they went to war 50 years ago, and demonstrate how difficult it would be to reunite Korean peninsula; most North Korean defectors never reach South Korea; they live under conditions in China almost equally as harsh as those they fled, with little or no hope of reaching South Korea; photo; map (M)

2000-04-24.title: A War Story's Missing Pages: Vietnam Forgets Those Who Lost
2000-04-24.abstract: Vietnam Today, occasional series on Vietnam 25 years after fall of Saigon, contrasts way Communist victors and members of America's South Vietnamese military allies are treated today; thousands of soldiers on victorious Communist side are buried in so-called 'martyrs' cemetery,' their tombstones surrounded by raked gravel paths and beds of flowers; formal ceremonies are held to honor them; in Ho Chi Minh City, formerly Saigon, there is abandoned military cemetery; dead there fought with South Vietnamese forces and were buried with honors before war ended; now many of their tombstones have been defaced or uprooted; Hanoi government continues to track down its 300,000 missing soldiers from war, but no effort is made to find soldiers missing from southern side; photos (L)

2000-04-24.title: Heroin Carries AIDS to a Region in Siberia
2000-04-24.abstract: Heroin is deadly catalyst in epidemic of HIV infections in Irkutsk, run-down river town of 650,000 in Siberia; region that hardly heard of AIDS year ago has recorded 5,000 new cases of HIV infection and registered more than 8,500 drug addicts, according to official statistics; true figures could be much greater; number of HIV-infected Russians is small so far--33,000 by official estimates, or 300,000 by international estimates--but potential for growth is huge; United Nations joined Russian officials to announce new effort to halt epidemic; virus's spread is accelerating and could move beyond drug users to heterosexual transmission without preventive measures; 40 percent of Russian prostitutes, who often use drugs, are HIV-positive; prostitution and drug use are thriving amid poverty and general breakdowns of law and mores following Soviet Union's collapse; photos; map (L)

2000-04-24.title: Micheline Glover, 76, a Bold Figure in the French Resistance
2000-04-24.abstract: Micheline Glover, who as 18-year-old girl worked for French Resistance during World War II, dies at age of 76 (M)

2000-04-24.title: UNIONS DENY STAND OVER TRADE POLICY IS PROTECTIONISM
2000-04-24.abstract: American labor movement seeks to fend off charges from many economists, business executives and members of Congress that its stand against trade expansion measures is protectionism; critics call labor's across-the-board fight against trade expansion crass effort to protect American jobs by preventing imports from increasing; union officials insist they have jettisoned their past protectionism; say they oppose trade initiatives because they fail to protect rights of foreign workers; AFL-CIO has been most powerful voice against China trade bill, helping sway scores of Democrats, including Richard A Gephardt, House Democratic leader, to oppose it; lawmakers say vote in House is too close to call, largely because of labor's aggressive opposition; labor's outspoken opposition is also complicating matters for Africa trade bill, which would reduce tariffs and quotas on imports from some of world's poorest countries; for three years, labor has blocked Congress's efforts to make it easier for Clinton administration to negotiate extending North American Free Trade Agreement to other countries (M)

2000-04-25.title: Study Says Combating Malaria Would Cost Little
2000-04-25.abstract: Study by Harvard University Center for International Development and London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine concludes that malaria hurts African economies more than has been recognized but could be better controlled for relatively little money; will be presented in Nigeria at first conference at which African heads of state will meet to discuss malaria; conference is sponsored by World Health Organization and begins on April 25; photo (M)

2000-04-25.title: Iran Tightens Crackdown On Reformist Publications
2000-04-25.abstract: TEHRAN—  The number of pro-reform newspapers and magazines forced to cease publication rose to 12 on Monday, as Iran's hard-liners moved to crush the progressive press and, with it, the move toward social and political change.

2000-04-25.title: Iran Reformers Feeling Pressed By Hard-Liners
